g.data-narration: Welcome to Lexical Issues in Java - the foundation of how Java reads and understands your code.
Lexical issues involve tokens - the smallest meaningful units in Java like identifiers, keywords, and operators.
g.text: Lexical analysis converts source code characters into meaningful units called
"tokens" - the building blocks of Java programs.

g.data-narration: Understanding lexical rules helps you avoid mysterious compilation errors and write better Java code.
g.text: Java's lexical rules form the foundation of the "Write Once, Run Anywhere" philosophy.
Mastering these concepts helps you understand and fix cryptic compilation errors.

g.data-narration: Java uses Unicode - a 16-bit character encoding that supports characters from virtually all writing systems worldwide.
g.text: Java uses Unicode - each character has a unique code point. char is 16-bit UTF-16.
Supports Chinese, Arabic, Hindi and more via surrogate pairs for characters outside BMP.

g.data-narration: Java provides Scanner, BufferedReader, and Console classes for reading input - each with different use cases.
g.text: Three main ways to read input: Scanner, BufferedReader, and Console.

g.data-narration: Scanner class provides nextInt(), nextDouble(), nextLine() methods but is slower due to regex processing.
BufferedReader is faster than Scanner - ideal for competitive programming and large inputs.
Tokenization breaks input into tokens - the smallest meaningful units like identifiers, numbers, and operators.
g.text: Breaking input into discrete tokens for processing by the compiler or application.

g.data-narration: White space separates tokens but is significant in literals. Comments are ignored by the compiler and cannot be nested.
Identifiers name variables and methods - must start with letter, dollar, or underscore. Cannot be Java keywords.
g.text: class, public, static, void, if, while, return, int, for...

g.data-narration: Literals are constant values - integers, floats, chars, strings, booleans. Each has specific prefixes and suffixes.
Operators perform operations - arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise. Understanding precedence is crucial.
Avoid these common errors: forgetting to close Scanner, mixing nextInt with nextLine, using lowercase L in long literals.
For exams: remember Unicode support, BufferedReader performance, identifier rules, and operator precedence.
g.text: Java uses Unicode, not just ASCII
L for long, f for float

g.data-narration: To summarize: Lexical issues cover Unicode, input handling, tokenization, identifiers, literals, and operators in Java.
g.text: ✓ BufferedReader is faster for competitive programming
✓ Literals: use L for long, f for float
university 2022 Scheme - Lexical Issues in Java - End of Topic
